# Extended Case References — Lecture XPwFzrNlgls

*Brief mentions of canonical cases that did not warrant a full `case_index.md` entry. Generated by reconciliation pass against the 1,676-cluster canon. Substantive treatments are in `case_index.md`.*

## Cases mentioned in passing

### China glass bridge high-heel impact fracture
- **canonical_cluster_id:** "China glass bridge high-heel impact fracture"
- **Anchor:** `§5.p3`
- **Quote (layer 3):** "Exactly. One of the big issues with glass in buildings is that there are multiple panels — it's called an IGU, an insulating glass unit. Think of it as a sandwich structure. There's that glass bridge in China that's been in the news this past year, you can walk on it thousands of feet in the air. In Chicago they have the ledge at the top of the [Sears Tower — speaker said Chrysler Building] where you can look down through the glass. With those types of glasses they do three-layer IGU. On the China bridge, they had a fracture in one of the panels, and the reason was they didn't tell people..."

### Nickel sulfide inclusion spontaneous glass breakage
- **canonical_cluster_id:** "Nickel sulfide inclusion spontaneous glass breakage"
- **Anchor:** `§5.p5`
- **Quote (layer 3):** "Wind loading — that's an important thing to take into account. ASTM, American Society of Testing Materials, has multiple committees that deal with load resistance and wind loading of glass structures. Another big issue is during installation — if there's some impact to the material during installation, even a slight nick removes the residual compressive stresses on the edge of the glass, and at some point it can break if it's not seated inside its encasement properly. That would be binding of the glass to the window frame — if it's not put in properly there's movement. Another one we'll talk..."

### Nickel sulfide inclusion spontaneous glass breakage
- **canonical_cluster_id:** "Nickel sulfide inclusion spontaneous glass breakage"
- **Anchor:** `§7.p2`
- **Quote (layer 3):** "Glass with a nickel sulfide inclusion has a characteristic figure-8 or butterfly breakage pattern. The reason nickel sulfide breakage is so scary is that it can happen spontaneously. You can have glass in service for 10 years and it can be fine, and one day it just breaks. The cause of that spontaneous breakage is the volume expansion between phase changes. Nickel sulfide has an alpha phase and a beta phase. When it transforms between those phases there's a volume change, and if the inclusion is at mid-thickness, that puts tensile stresses into the glass as it expands. Glass doesn't work well..."
